content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission


March 21, 1990


Larry Israel
1625 Olympic Blvd.
Santa Monica, CA 90404

Re: Your Request For Advice
Our File No. A-90-125

Dear Mr. Israel:

You have requested advice concerning the campaign disclosure
provisions of the Political Reform Act.1/

QUESTION

What method should be used to determine the value of air
transportation provided to a candidate for purposes of receiving
adequate payment from the candidate?

CONCLUSION

The candidate must pay the fair market value of the air
transportation, which can be determined by using the commercial
air rate or the charter rate divided by the number of passengers.
If less than the fair market value is paid for the service, a non-
monetary contribution has been made to the candidate.

FACTS

You are a pilot and own an airplane. You may provide a
candidate for state office with air transportation to various
speaking engagements.




1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Larry Israel
Page Two

ANALYSIS

The term "contribution" includes:

...a payment except to the extent that full and adequate
consideration is received unless it is clear from the
surrounding circumstances that it is not made for political
purposes. (Section 82015.)

"Full and adequate consideration" as used in Section 82015
means the "fair market value," or whatever it would cost the
candidate to obtain the same or similar services on the open
market. (Section 82025.5.) In In re Stone (1977) 3 FPPC Ops. 52
at 58 (copy enclosed), the Commission stated that the fair market
value of private air transportation can be determined by using
either the commercial air rate or the charter rate divided by the
number of passengers.

If a candidate pays less than the fair market value for the
air transportation you provide, you have made a non-monetary
contribution to the candidate.

The Act places limitations on contributions to candidates for
elective office based on a fiscal year (July 1 - June 30).
(Sections 85301-85303.) Monetary and non-monetary contributions
as well as loans and enforceable promises from a "person" to a
candidate are limited to $1,000 per fiscal year. (Section 85301.)
A "person" includes an individual, corporation, proprietorship,
firm, partnership or company. (Section 85102(b).)

If you have any questions regarding this letter, please
contact me at (916) 322-5662.

Sincerely,

Kathryn E. Donovan
General Counsel

[signature]
By: Alice Hughes
Political Reform Consultant
Technical Assistance and
Analysis Division

Enclosure
